subject: Mathematics
q: The area under the curve $y=|\cos x-\sin x|$, $0 \leq x \leq \frac{\pi}{2}$, and above $x$-axis is :
question: The area under the curve $y=|\cos x-\sin x|$, $0 \leq x \leq \frac{\pi}{2}$, and above $x$-axis is :
solution:
$$ \text { } y=|\cos x-\sin x| $$
$$ \begin{aligned} \text { Required area } & =2 \int_0^{\pi / 4}(\cos x-\sin x) d x \\ & =2\left[\sin x+\cos x 0_0^{\pi / 4}\right] \\ & =2\left[\frac{2}{\sqrt{2}}-1\right]=(2 \sqrt{2}-2) \text { sq. units } \end{aligned} $$

subject: Mathematics
q: The area of the region $R = \{(x, y): xy \leq 27, 1 \leq y \leq x^2\}$ is equal to:
question: The area of the region $R = \{(x, y): xy \leq 27, 1 \leq y \leq x^2\}$ is equal to:
solution:
The given region is bounded by the curves $y = 1$, $y = x^2$, and $xy = 27$ in the first quadrant.

Let us find the points of intersection of these curves:
Intersection of $y = 1$ and $y = x^2$ gives $x = 1$.
Intersection of $y = x^2$ and $xy = 27$ gives $x(x^2) = 27 \Rightarrow x^3 = 27 \Rightarrow x = 3$, so $y = 9$.
Intersection of $y = 1$ and $xy = 27$ gives $x = 27$.

We can find the area by integrating with respect to $y$ from $y = 1$ to $y = 9$. For a given $y$, the value of $x$ ranges from the parabola $x = \sqrt{y}$ to the hyperbola $x = \dfrac{27}{y}$.

The area $A$ is given by:
$A = \int_{1}^{9} \left( \dfrac{27}{y} - \sqrt{y} \right) dy$

Integrating the terms:
$A = \left[ 27 \ln y - \dfrac{2}{3} y^{3/2} \right]_{1}^{9}$

Substituting the limits:
$A = \left( 27 \ln 9 - \dfrac{2}{3} (9)^{3/2} \right) - \left( 27 \ln 1 - \dfrac{2}{3} (1)^{3/2} \right)$

$A = \left( 27 \ln(3^2) - \dfrac{2}{3} (27) \right) - \left( 0 - \dfrac{2}{3} \right)$

$A = 54 \ln 3 - 18 + \dfrac{2}{3}$

$A = 54 \ln 3 - \dfrac{54}{3} + \dfrac{2}{3}$

$A = 54 \ln 3 - \dfrac{52}{3}$

Answer: $54\log_e 3 - \dfrac{52}{3}$

subject: Mathematics
q: If the area of the region $\left\{(x, y): 1-2 x \leqslant y \leqslant 4-x^{2}, x \geqslant 0, y \geqslant 0\right\}$ is $\frac{\alpha}{\beta}, \alpha, \beta \in \mathbf{N}, \operatorname{gcd}(\alpha, \beta)=1$, then the value of $(\alpha+\beta)$ is :
question: If the area of the region $\left\{(x, y): 1-2 x \leqslant y \leqslant 4-x^{2}, x \geqslant 0, y \geqslant 0\right\}$ is $\frac{\alpha}{\beta}, \alpha, \beta \in \mathbf{N}, \operatorname{gcd}(\alpha, \beta)=1$, then the value of $(\alpha+\beta)$ is :
solution:
Required area $= \dfrac{2}{3} \times 8 - \dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{1}{2} \times 1$
$= \dfrac{16}{3} - \dfrac{1}{4} = \dfrac{61}{12} = \dfrac{\alpha}{\beta}$
$\Rightarrow \alpha + \beta = 73$

Given curve is y2=4x-2 and line y=2x-8

Finding the point of intersection of y=2x-4 and y2=4x.

⇒x=y+82=y2+84

⇒2y+16=y2+8

⇒y2-2y-8=0

⇒y-4y+2=0

⇒y=4,-2

⇒x=6,3

Area of the bounded region is given by, A=∫-24y+82-y24+2dy

⇒A=y24+2y-y312-24

⇒A=4+8-163-1+4-23

⇒A=9 sq. units.

q: One of the points of intersection of the curves $y=1+3 x-2 x^2$ and $y=\frac{1}{x}$ is $\left(\frac{1}{2}, 2\right)$. Let the area of the region enclosed by these curves be $\frac{1}{24}(l \sqrt{5}+\mathrm{m})-\mathrm{n} \log _{\mathrm{e}}(1+\sqrt{5})$, where $l, \mathrm{~m}, \mathrm{n} \in \mathbf{N}$. Then $l+\mathrm{m}+\mathrm{n}$ is equal to
question: One of the points of intersection of the curves $y=1+3 x-2 x^2$ and $y=\frac{1}{x}$ is $\left(\frac{1}{2}, 2\right)$. Let the area of the region enclosed by these curves be $\frac{1}{24}(l \sqrt{5}+\mathrm{m})-\mathrm{n} \log _{\mathrm{e}}(1+\sqrt{5})$, where $l, \mathrm{~m}, \mathrm{n} \in \mathbf{N}$. Then $l+\mathrm{m}+\mathrm{n}$ is equal to
solution: $\begin{aligned} & A=\int_{\frac{1}{2}}^{\frac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2}}\left(1+3 x-2 x^2-\frac{1}{x}\right) \mathrm{dx} \\ & A=\left[x+\frac{3 x^2}{2}-\frac{2 x^3}{3}-\ln x\right]_{\frac{1}{2}}^{1+\sqrt{5}} \\ & A=\frac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2}+\frac{3}{2}\left(\frac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2}\right)^2-\frac{2}{3}\left(\frac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2}\right)^3-\ln \left(\frac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2}\right) \\ & -\frac{1}{2}-\frac{3}{2}\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)+\frac{2}{3}\left(\frac{1}{8}\right)+\ln \left(\frac{1}{2}\right) \\ & A=\frac{1}{2}+\frac{\sqrt{5}}{2}+\frac{3}{8}+\frac{3}{4} \sqrt{5}+\frac{15}{8}-\frac{4}{3}-\frac{2}{3} \sqrt{5} \\ & -\frac{1}{2}-\frac{3}{8}+\frac{1}{12}-\ln (1+\sqrt{5}) \\ & =\sqrt{5}\left(\frac{1}{2}+\frac{3}{4}-\frac{2}{3}\right)+\frac{15}{8}-\frac{4}{3}+\frac{1}{12}-\ln (1+\sqrt{5}) \\ & =\frac{14}{24} \sqrt{5}+\frac{15}{24}-\ln (1+\sqrt{5})\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q:
If the area of the region
$\left\{(x, y):\left|4-x^2\right| \leq y \leq x^2, y \leq 4, x \geq 0\right\}$
is $\left(\frac{80 \sqrt{2}}{\alpha}-\beta\right), \boldsymbol{\alpha}, \boldsymbol{\beta} \in \mathbf{N}$, then $\alpha+\beta$ is equal to _______.
question:
If the area of the region
$\left\{(x, y):\left|4-x^2\right| \leq y \leq x^2, y \leq 4, x \geq 0\right\}$
is $\left(\frac{80 \sqrt{2}}{\alpha}-\beta\right), \boldsymbol{\alpha}, \boldsymbol{\beta} \in \mathbf{N}$, then $\alpha+\beta$ is equal to _______.
solution:
$\mathrm{A}=\int_0^4 \sqrt{4+\mathrm{y}} \mathrm{dy}-\int_0^2 \sqrt{4-\mathrm{y}} \mathrm{dy}-\int_2^4 \sqrt{\mathrm{y}} \mathrm{dy}$
$=\left(\frac{(4+y)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{\frac{3}{2}}\right)_0^4+\left(\frac{(4-y)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{\frac{3}{2}}\right)_0^2-\left(\frac{\mathrm{y}^{\frac{3}{2}}}{\frac{3}{2}}\right)_0^4$
$\frac{80 \sqrt{2}}{3}-16=\frac{40 \sqrt{2}}{3}-16$
$\alpha=6, \beta=16$
$\alpha+\beta=22$

subject: Mathematics
q: If the area of the region $\left\{(x, y): \frac{\mathrm{a}}{x^2} \leq y \leq \frac{1}{x}, 1 \leq x \leq 2,0 < \mathrm{a} < 1\right\}$ is $\left(\log _{\mathrm{e}} 2\right)-\frac{1}{7}$ then the value of $7 \mathrm{a}-3$ is equal to:
question: If the area of the region $\left\{(x, y): \frac{\mathrm{a}}{x^2} \leq y \leq \frac{1}{x}, 1 \leq x \leq 2,0 < \mathrm{a} < 1\right\}$ is $\left(\log _{\mathrm{e}} 2\right)-\frac{1}{7}$ then the value of $7 \mathrm{a}-3$ is equal to:
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { area } \int_1^2\left(\frac{1}{x}-\frac{a}{x^2}\right) d x \\ & {\left[\ell n x+\frac{a}{x}\right]_1^2} \\ & \ell \operatorname{nn} 2+\frac{a}{2}-a=\log _e 2-\frac{1}{7} \\ & \frac{-a}{2}=-\frac{1}{7} \\ & a=\frac{2}{7} \\ & 7 a=2 \\ & 7 a-3=-1\end{aligned}$

question: The area of the region enclosed by the curves $y=x, x=e, y=\frac{1}{x}$ and the positive $x$-axis is

⇒16x+22-y-22=16

Hence, equation of conjugate axis will be x=-2 and equation of transverse axis is given by y=2,

q: The area of the region $\{(x, y) : x^2 - 8x \leq y \leq -x\}$ is :
question: The area of the region $\{(x, y) : x^2 - 8x \leq y \leq -x\}$ is :
solution:
The given region is bounded by the parabola $y = x^2 - 8x$ and the line $y = -x$.

To find the points of intersection, equate the two equations:

$x^2 - 8x = -x$

$x^2 - 7x = 0$

$x(x - 7) = 0$

The points of intersection are $x = 0$ and $x = 7$.

In the interval $[0, 7]$, the line $y = -x$ lies above the parabola $y = x^2 - 8x$.

The required area $A$ is given by:

$A = \int_{0}^{7} (-x - (x^2 - 8x)) dx$

$A = \int_{0}^{7} (7x - x^2) dx$

Evaluating the integral:

$A = \left[ \dfrac{7x^2}{2} - \dfrac{x^3}{3} \right]_{0}^{7}$

$A = \dfrac{7(49)}{2} - \dfrac{343}{3}$

$A = \dfrac{343}{2} - \dfrac{343}{3}$

$A = 343 \left( \dfrac{1}{2} - \dfrac{1}{3} \right)$

$A = \dfrac{343}{6}$

Answer: $\dfrac{343}{6}$

subject: Mathematics
q: If the area of the region $\left\{(x, y):-1 \leq x \leq 1,0 \leq y \leq a+\mathrm{e}^{|x|}-\mathrm{e}^{-x}, \mathrm{a}\gt0\right\}$ is $\frac{\mathrm{e}^2+8 \mathrm{e}+1}{\mathrm{e}}$, then the value of $a$ is :
question: If the area of the region $\left\{(x, y):-1 \leq x \leq 1,0 \leq y \leq a+\mathrm{e}^{|x|}-\mathrm{e}^{-x}, \mathrm{a}\gt0\right\}$ is $\frac{\mathrm{e}^2+8 \mathrm{e}+1}{\mathrm{e}}$, then the value of $a$ is :
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & y \in\left[0, a+e^{|x|}-e^{-x}\right] \\ & \text { (i) If } x \geq 0 \Rightarrow y \in\left(0, a+e^x-\frac{1}{e^x}\right) \\ & \text { if } x < 0 \Rightarrow y \in\left(0, a+e^{-x}-e^{-x}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow y \in(0, a)\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \text { Area }=(a)+\int_0^1\left(a+e^x-e^{-x}\right) d x=\frac{e^2+8 e+1}{e} \\ & =a+\left.\left(a x+e^x+e^{-x}\right)\right|_0 ^1=e+8+\frac{1}{e} \\ & =a+\left(a+e+\frac{1}{e}-2\right)=e+\frac{1}{e}+8 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 a-2=8 \Rightarrow a=5\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: If a straight line $y-x=2$ divides the region $x^2+y^2 \leq 4$ into two parts, then the ratio of the area of the smaller part to the area of the greater part is
question: If a straight line $y-x=2$ divides the region $x^2+y^2 \leq 4$ into two parts, then the ratio of the area of the smaller part to the area of the greater part is
solution:
Let I be the smaller portion and II be the greater portion of the given figure then,
Area of $I=\int_{-2}^0\left[\sqrt{4-x^2}-(x+2] d x\right.$ $$ \begin{aligned} & =\left[\frac{x}{2} \sqrt{4-x^2}+\frac{4}{2} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)\right]_{-2}^0-\left[\frac{x^2}{2}+2 x\right]_{-2}^0 \\ & =\left[2 \sin ^{-1}(-1)\right]-\left[-\frac{4}{2}+4\right]=2 \times \frac{\pi}{2}-2=\pi-2 \end{aligned} $$ Now, area of $\mathrm{II}=$ Area of circle $-$ area of $\mathrm{I}$. $$ \begin{aligned} & =4 \pi-(\pi-2) \\ & =3 \pi+2 \end{aligned} $$ Hence, required ratio $=\frac{\text { area of I }}{\text { area of II }}=\frac{\pi-2}{3 \pi+2}$

subject: Mathematics
q:

$\left\{(x, y): 0 \leq \mathrm{y} \leq 2|x|+1,0 \leq \mathrm{y} \leq x^2+1,|x| \leq 3\right\}$ is
question:
The area (in sq. units) of the region
$\left\{(x, y): 0 \leq \mathrm{y} \leq 2|x|+1,0 \leq \mathrm{y} \leq x^2+1,|x| \leq 3\right\}$ is
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { Area }=2\left[\int_0^2\left(x^2+1\right) d x+\frac{1}{2}[5+7] \times 1\right] \\ & =\frac{64}{3}\end{aligned}$

q: The odd natural number a, such that the area of the region bounded by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mi>a</mi></msup></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>364</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math>, equal to:

Plotting the graph of the given functions, we get the shaded region as the area bounded by the curves.

Given, area of shaded region =3643

⇒ya+1a+113=3643

⇒3a+1-1a+1=3643=729-16=36-16

⇒a=5

q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>b</mi></math> respectively be the points of local maximum and local minimum of the function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>12</mn><mi>x</mi></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is the total area of the region bounded by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>f</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>,</mo></math> the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis and the lines <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>a</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">=</mo><mi>b</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">,</mo></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is equal to ______.
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>b</mi></math> respectively be the points of local maximum and local minimum of the function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>12</mn><mi>x</mi></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is the total area of the region bounded by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>f</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>,</mo></math> the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis and the lines <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>a</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">=</mo><mi>b</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">,</mo></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is equal to ______.
solution:
.f'(x)=6x2-6x-12=6x2-x-2

=6(x-2)(x+1)

Clearly, f'-1->0 & f'-1+<0

Hence, x=-1 is the point of local maximum.

Also, f'2-<0 & f'2+>0

Hence, x=2 is the point of local minimum.

∴a=-1 & b=2

Points=2,-20 & -1,7

A=∫-102x3-3x2-12xdx-∫022x3-3x2-12xdx

=x42-x3-6x2-10-x42-x3-6x202

=92+24=572⇒4 A=114

q: Let $f(x)$ be a non-negative continuous function such that the area bounded by the curve $y=f(x), x$-axis and the ordinates $x=\frac{\pi}{4}$ and $x=\beta>\frac{\pi}{4}$ is $\left(\beta \sin \beta+\frac{\pi}{4} \cos \beta+\sqrt{2} \beta\right)$. Then $f\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)$ is
question: Let $f(x)$ be a non-negative continuous function such that the area bounded by the curve $y=f(x), x$-axis and the ordinates $x=\frac{\pi}{4}$ and $x=\beta>\frac{\pi}{4}$ is $\left(\beta \sin \beta+\frac{\pi}{4} \cos \beta+\sqrt{2} \beta\right)$. Then $f\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)$ is
solution:
Given that $\int_{\pi / 4}^\beta f(x) d x=\beta \sin \beta+\frac{\pi}{4} \cos \beta+2 \beta$
Differentiating w. r. t $\beta$
$f(\beta)=\beta \cos \beta+\sin \beta-\frac{\pi}{4} \sin \beta+\sqrt{2}$
$f\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)=\left(1-\frac{\pi}{4}\right) \sin \frac{\pi}{2}+\sqrt{2}=1-\frac{\pi}{2}+\sqrt{2}$.

q: The area (in sq. units) of the region bounded by the curves <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> in the upper half plane is _________.
question: The area (in sq. units) of the region bounded by the curves <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> in the upper half plane is _________.
solution:
The given curves are x2+2y-1=0, y2+4x-4=0 and y2-4x-4=0.

Drawing their graphs, we get 

The required area is shown by the shaded part and since the curves y2+4x-4=0 and y2-4x-4=0 are symmetric about the y-axis, hence they have the same area.

Hence, the required area=2∫024-y24dy-∫011-x22dx

=2∫02144-y2dy-∫01121-x2dx

=2144y-y3302-12x-x3301

=2148-83-0-121-13-0

=243-13=2sq.units .

question: The area of the region given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="{" close="}"><mrow><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>y</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>:</mo><mi>x</mi><mi>y</mi><mo>≤</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>≤</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>≤</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced></math> is :
solution:
Given,

xy≤8 & 1≤y≤x2

On solving above curve we get intersecting point as 2,4

Now on plotting the diagram of the given curves we get,

Required area

A=∫12x2-1dx+∫288x-1dx

⇒A=∫12x2dx+∫288xdx-∫18dx

⇒A=x3312+8lnx28-x18

⇒A=8ln4-143 

⇒A=16ln2-143 sq. units

subject: Mathematics
q: Let the area of the bounded region $\left\{(x, y): 0 \leq 9 x \leq y^2, y \geq 3 x-6\right\}$ be A. Then 6 A is equal to ________
question: Let the area of the bounded region $\left\{(x, y): 0 \leq 9 x \leq y^2, y \geq 3 x-6\right\}$ be A. Then 6 A is equal to ________
solution:
$0 \leq 9 x \leq y^2 \& y \geq 3 x-6$

$\begin{aligned} & A=\text { Required Area }=\left[\int_0^1(-3 \sqrt{x}) d x-\int_0^1(3 x-6) d x\right] \\ & A=-\left.3\left(\frac{x^{\frac{3}{2}}}{\frac{3}{2}}\right)\right|_0 ^1-\left.\left(\frac{3 x^2}{2}-6 x\right)\right|_0 ^1 \\ & A=-2[1-0]\left[\frac{3}{2}-6\right] \\ & A=-2-\frac{3}{2}+6=\frac{5}{2} \text { Sq. unit } \\ & \therefore 6 A=6 \times \frac{5}{2}=15\end{aligned}$
